results.possibleIdentity: Walter Ungerer (1935–2024) was an American experimental filmmaker, media artist, and painter. He was a significant figure in the New York City underground and avant-garde film movement during the 1960s. Ungerer studied art and architecture at the Pratt Institute (BFA, 1958) and later attended Columbia University (MA and PD, 1964), where he began his transition from painting to filmmaking. He was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, appearing in contexts alongside other E.A.T. artists and collaborators such as Stan VanDerBeek and Robert Rauschenberg. His early films, including 'The Tasmanian Devil' (1965), 'Meet Me, Jesus' (1966), and 'A Lion’s Tale' (1968), gained him national recognition. In 1969, his work 'Ubi Est Terram Oobiae?' was part of a worldwide touring program organized by the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA). After moving to Vermont in 1969, he founded the non-profit production company Dark Horse Films in 1976 to produce experimental narrative features such as 'The Animal' (1976) and 'The House Without Steps' (1978). In his later career, he relocated to Maine (2003) and focused on digital media and computer-assisted art. His work has been exhibited at major institutions including MoMA, the Tate, and the Venice Biennale.

results.possibleIdentity: Aaron Marcus (born May 22, 1943) is a pioneering American graphic designer, artist, and user-interface expert who was among the first to integrate computer technology into visual art and design. He received a B.A. in Physics from Princeton University (1965) and an M.F.A. in Graphic Design from Yale University (1968). In 1967, Marcus became the first professional graphic designer to work full-time in computer graphics during a summer internship at AT&T Bell Laboratories. Between 1968 and 1977, he was a faculty member at Princeton University, teaching in the School of Architecture and Urban Planning and the Visual Arts Program. During the late 1960s, Marcus was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, and his work was featured in the E.A.T.-organized exhibition 'Some More Beginnings' at the Brooklyn Museum (1968–1969). His artistic and technical achievements during the 1970s include developing a prototype desktop publishing system for the AT&T Picturephone (1969–1971), creating 'Cybernetic Landscape I' (1971–1973)—considered one of the first virtual reality art environments—and producing computer-assisted 'poem-drawings.' Other notable early works include 'Evolving Gravity' and 'Noise Barrier.' In 1982, he founded Aaron Marcus and Associates (AM+A), an early computer-based design firm specializing in user interfaces and information visualization.
